title: Device-Agnostic Microwave Noise Metrology for Nonlinear Cryogenic Quantum Devices
authors: Andrea Celotto, Alessandro Alocco, Bernardo Galvano, Luca Fasolo, Emanuele Palumbo, Luca Callegaro, Luca Oberto, Patrizia Livreri, Emanuele Enrico
abstract: Microwave devices capable of near-quantum-limited signal processing are essential components in the toolbox of solid-state quantum technologies. The manipulation and readout of single-photon microwave signals through amplifiers, mixers, isolators, etc. must fulfill strict requirements in terms of signal integrity to ensure reliable operation.   These active microwave quantum devices operate in complex cryo-electronic setups. This poses challenges to their characterization, since all relevant figures of merit must be expressed at the reference planes of their ports. Even though cryogenic S-parameter calibration is non-trivial, metrological approaches are converging toward rigorous methods. Furthermore, preserving signal integrity must be quantified via absolute noise levels at the ports of the Device Under Test (DUT), requiring an absolute power reference.   In this work, we present an in situ noise metrology protocol based on substituting a controllable noise source for the DUT. We motivate this choice by showing that placing the noise source at the DUT input impacts the separability of the calibration from the DUT characteristics.   Our proposed architecture combines Planck spectroscopy using a Variable Temperature Stage with Short-Open-Load-Reciprocal scattering-parameter calibration, so that noise and scattering quantities are referred to the same cryogenic reference planes. In this configuration, the readout-chain calibration is separated from the internal dynamics of the DUT. As a demanding use case, we apply the protocol to a Josephson Traveling Wave Parametric Amplifier and extract its gain and input-referred added noise under pump conditions activating multimode nonlinear behavior. This illustrates how our device-agnostic protocol supports portable noise characterization of nonlinear cryogenic microwave devices.
summary: The paper presents a new method for measuring the noise and gain of cryogenic microwave devices by substituting the device with a known thermal noise source. This approach avoids the errors caused by the device's own nonlinearities during the calibration process. It was successfully tested on a nonlinear traveling-wave parametric amplifier.
main_problem: Accurately characterizing the gain and added noise of nonlinear cryogenic microwave devices, such as JTWPAs, is difficult because traditional serial measurement configurations introduce model-dependent biases and cannot easily decouple the device's internal dynamics from the readout chain.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a device-agnostic substitution-based metrology protocol that successfully extracts the gain and input-referred added noise of a JTWPA even under complex, pump-dependent nonlinear regimes.
method: The protocol uses a substitution method where a controllable noise source (from a Variable Temperature Stage) is switched into the measurement path to replace the DUT, combined with Planck spectroscopy and SOLR S-parameter calibration.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of a dilution refrigerator setup with a Variable Temperature Stage (VTS) and a Josephson Traveling Wave Parametric Amplifier (JTWPA) operating in the C and X bands at millikelvin temperatures.
key_observables: Power Spectral Density (PSD), S-parameters (gain, transmission, reflection), and input-referred added noise.
important_parameters: Pump power, signal frequency, system gain, system noise temperature, and the quantum-limited noise floor.
main_assumptions: Assumes the noise source can be treated as a two-port component and that the electron temperature in the VTS follows the temperature of the copper body.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a flow diagram of photon detection; Figure 2 compares serial vs. substitution configurations; Figure 3 displays JTWPA gain and noise as functions of pump power; Figure 4 shows statistical bias in regression models.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of calibration bias, proposes a substitution-based architecture, details the integration of SOLR and Planck spectroscopy, validates the method using a nonlinear JTWPA, and provides a mathematical analysis of potential errors.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems researchers as it provides a rigorous way to characterize the noise and signal integrity of nonlinear microwave components essential for quantum readout and signal processing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Flow diagram (not at scale) of the scattering of fluctuations impacting the output signal mode, tracked across relevant experimental stages. Photons are picked up from thermal baths due to dissipative processes. The gain G can be identified as the ratio of the final and initial widths of the uppermost flow of photons inside the device.

Fig. 2. Two placements of the absolute noise reference, labeled REF. (a) In a serial configuration the DUT is included in the path between the noise source and the detector. (b) In a substitution configuration the noise source and the DUT are connected to the readout chain through cryogenic switches. The reference planes Rj indicate where the relevant microwave quantities are defined. The female-female thru connection can symbolize a more complete S-parameter calibration calkit consisting also of impedance standards.

Fig. 3. (a,b) Gain G and added noise Nadd of the JTWPA as functions of pump power Pp and signal frequency. The frequency-dependent fine features in the gain, commonly referred to as ripple, are deterministic features attributed to standing waves in the device packaging rather than measurement noise, as their consistency for various pump powers may suggest. (c) Gain and added noise, averaged over the 3.6–5.5 GHz band, as functions of pump power. The colored band around the noise curve represents its associated uncertainty, propagated from the fit on Planck-spectroscopy data.

Fig. 4. Illustration of the bias described in Appendix A based on synthetic but realistical data. The solid curve is the nonlinear model of Eq. (12a); the dots are sampled calibration points; the dashed line is the simplified affine regression. The clear structure of residuals to the fit shows the consequence of neglecting energy-dependent contributions δN(E).

Fig. 5. Mechanical rendering of the custom VTS assembly. The internal cavity houses a matched attenuator, a 100 Ωheater, and a ruthenium-oxide thermometer. The VTS is mounted on the sample space from below, anchored through stainless-steel screws. Alumina washers are used to contol the physical distance of the object from the samples mounted above.
